dating.basis: No contemporary records; dated by linguistic references (mentions Greek script 'yavanānī' on some readings), citations by later grammarians, and relative chronology with the Buddhist canon.

positions.holders: Cardona and much of modern Indology
positions.evidence: The yavanānī reference and relative chronology with Kātyāyana and Patañjali.

positions.holders: Goldstücker and older scholarship; some Indian traditional accounts place him earlier still
positions.evidence: Absence of Buddhist vocabulary; traditional biographies. Reported as tradition, not as an established date.

summary: Grammarian of Śalātura in Gandhāra (near modern Lahor village, Swabi District, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan). His Aṣṭādhyāyī compresses the whole of Sanskrit grammar into ~4,000 sūtras: an ordered system of rewrite rules with a defined metalanguage, abbreviation conventions (pratyāhāras), default-and-override rule ordering, and recursion — a generative grammar, two and a half millennia before the term existed.
whyItMatters: The Aṣṭādhyāyī is the earliest surviving formal system for describing a language completely. Leonard Bloomfield called it 'one of the greatest monuments of human intelligence.' Modern generative linguistics and the rule-based formalisms of computer science recognize their own methods in it.
honestyNote: Pāṇini codified Sanskrit — he did not invent it; his grammar presupposes the Vedic corpus and cites earlier grammarians (Śākaṭāyana, Sphoṭāyana...). His birthplace lies in present-day Pakistan; he belongs to the Sanskrit-Vedic tradition his work describes. Both facts are recorded here — see the ledger entry on 'ancient Pakistani grammarian' framing.

locations.name: Śalātura (Lahor, Swabi District, KPK, Pakistan) — birthplace
locations.note: approximate; identified from Xuanzang's itinerary and the toponym

locations.name: Taxila (Takṣaśilā), Punjab, Pakistan — the learning hub of Gandhāra
locations.note: association traditional; see ledger on the 'Taxila university' framing

chain: Aṣṭādhyāyī: ordered rewrite rules + metalanguage + recursion fully specify Sanskrit
19th-c. European linguistics absorbs Pāṇini (Böhtlingk's edition, 1887); Bloomfield and structuralism build on Pāṇinian method — a documented influence on linguistics
Backus–Naur Form (1959) for ALGOL is formally equivalent in power to Pāṇini's rule notation; BNF was developed independently
Ingerman (CACM, 1967) proposed renaming BNF 'Pāṇini–Backus Form' in recognition of the anticipation

sources.label: Ingerman, P.Z., "'Pāṇini-Backus Form' Suggested", Communications of the ACM 10:3 (1967)
Kiparsky, P., "On the Architecture of Pāṇini's Grammar" (2009)
Staal, F., "Euclid and Pāṇini", Philosophy East and West 15 (1965)
Cardona, G., Pāṇini: A Survey of Research (Mouton, 1976)
Bloomfield, L., Language (1933), ch. 1 — the 'monuments' assessment
Kiparsky, P., "On the Architecture of Pāṇini's Grammar" (2009)
Scharfe, H., Education in Ancient India (Brill, 2002) — on Gandhāran learning and what Taxila was and wasn't
Xuanzang, Records of the Western World (tr. Beal) — Śalātura identification

dharmicTie.statement: Vyākaraṇa is a vedāṅga: the tradition's own stated purpose for grammar is rakṣā — protection of the Veda. Pāṇini's grammar contains dedicated rules for Vedic (chandas) forms; severing him from this corpus (see ledger) erases what the Aṣṭādhyāyī is for.

scriptureRefs.text: rakṣā — preservation of the Veda — named first among grammar's purposes
chandaḥ pādau tu vedasya... — the vedāṅgas as the Veda's limbs
